turns.content: I'm reading an exercise about packing a struct to save memory, but I thought adding #[repr(packed)] would just shrink every field by half. Why is that wrong?

That assumption misses the point of alignment rules. The compiler still enforces byte boundaries for each field based on its type's natural alignment, like how a usize or f64 needs specific padding before it. #[repr(packed)] removes that internal padding between fields but doesn't magically change the size requirement of the individual types themselves.
So if I have a u16 and an i32, packing them should just put them next to each other without any gap? That seems like it would save bytes compared to normal layout.

You're right about the gap between fields being removed, but you need to check the total size with size_of::<MyStruct>() because the first field still needs its own alignment. If your struct starts with a u16 on a 4-byte boundary system, that initial padding might remain even in packed mode depending on how the compiler interprets the start.
Wait, so #[repr(packed)] isn't actually making my struct smaller than I thought? Can you show me a concrete example where it fails?

Try defining a struct with a u16 followed by an f32 without padding, then print the size of that packed variant versus the normal one. Often the difference is just two bytes instead of four because the first field still consumes its initial alignment space.
I'm trying to build a high-throughput packet parser in Rust but I keep hitting alignment penalties. Should I just use #[repr(packed)] everywhere or is there a better pattern for struct layout?

Using #[repr(packed)] blindly can actually hurt performance because it disables automatic alignment, which forces the CPU to do extra work on cache misses. A better approach is to use #[repr(C)] with manual field reordering so that frequently accessed data sits at natural boundaries without padding, and then verify the layout matches your expectations using size_of! assertions before you allocate buffers.
Okay, I tried to manually reorder fields in my struct but now size_of! says the total is bigger than expected. Is that normal for #[repr(C)]?

That's exactly what you want to see if you're doing it right. When you use #[repr(C)], Rust guarantees a C-compatible layout, which means the compiler inserts padding between fields that aren't aligned to their natural boundaries. If your struct has an i32 followed by a u64, the i32 might need 4 bytes of padding before the u64 starts at an 8-byte boundary, so the size will be larger than the raw sum of field sizes.
So I should avoid #[repr(packed)] for performance and stick with #[repr(C)]? But what if I need to reduce padding anyway?

You can use #[repr(C)] but strategically place smaller fields after larger ones, or group related data together so the compiler minimizes unnecessary padding. If you absolutely must minimize layout overhead for a specific protocol, then yes, consider #[repr(packed)] but be aware that this will cost you in runtime alignment checks and cache efficiency unless your data access patterns perfectly match the packed layout.
Got it. One last thing, does #[align(N)] work on top of #[repr(C)] or do I need to choose between them?

You can combine #[repr(C)] and #[align(N)] but only for the entire struct, not individual fields. Combining them with field-level attributes doesn't make sense because #[repr(C)] already dictates alignment based on the most strictly aligned field in the struct. Use #[align(N)] when you want to force a specific minimum alignment across all instances of that type without relying on automatic padding calculations.

I've been trying to port a legacy C library into Rust, and I'm noticing that the memory usage is higher than expected. It feels just like when I first learned about struct packing in C, where reordering fields could save bytes of padding.

That intuition is spot on; Rust gives you precise control over this via the repr attribute system. You can use #[repr(C)] to match C layout exactly or #[repr(packed)] to eliminate all internal padding, which often shrinks your structs dramatically.

I was thinking I could just remove padding by declaring my struct as packed without changing the field order. My assumption is that packing will automatically align everything perfectly regardless of how I arrange the fields inside the struct definition.

That's a common mistake; #[repr(packed)] does remove padding between individual fields, but it doesn't guarantee optimal alignment for larger data types like arrays or large integers. If you have an array of u64 inside your struct, the compiler still needs to align that entire block to 8 bytes even when packed.
I see now; so field order matters because different types have different alignment requirements? Like if I put a u64 before a i32, the layout changes compared to putting them in reverse?

Exactly. Alignment dictates how much space must be skipped between fields based on their size and type attributes. A u64 requires 8-byte alignment while an i32 only needs 4 bytes, so swapping them changes where padding lands in the memory layout.
So if I want to minimize total size, I should put smaller aligned types first and larger ones last? What about using #[repr(align(N))] to force specific alignment without extra padding?

You can use #[repr(align(N))] to enforce a minimum alignment on the entire struct, which prevents accidental misalignment from breaking ABI compatibility. However, remember that size_of! assertions will tell you exactly how much padding exists after applying these attributes.
